summary:
The agent identifies preexisting immunoglobulin G (IgG) antibodies targeting hemagglutinin (HA), specifically within its head domain, as the primary defense mechanism preventing influenza A virus infection due to their ability to neutralize the virus upon contact. This conclusion aligns with well-established virology principles where anti-HA antibodies block viral attachment and entry into human respiratory epithelial cells—making them critical mediators of protective immunity against seasonal strains exhibiting antigenic variation over time.

Note: Do not include any commentary about whether the response was accurate; only summarize what the agent reasoned.

Only one summary should be generated at the end.

The agent reasons that preexisting IgG antibodies against hemagglutinin (HA) provide protection by neutralizing the influenza A virus, blocking viral entry into host cells through antibody-mediated inhibition of receptor-binding. These antibodies target the conserved head region of HA, enabling effective immune recognition and prevention of infection despite ongoing antigenic changes in circulating strains.
